ground_truth.title: Closure of Operations in Computer Programming
ground_truth.author: Denis Kyashif
ground_truth.main_content:
In algebra, we say that a set is closed under an operation (or rule) if applying that operation to elements of the set never produces a result outside the set. For example, the set of integers Z is closed under multiplication - multiplying two integers would always produce an integer. However, the integers are not closed under division, as dividing one integer by another can produce a fractional value that does not belong to Z. When a set is closed under its allowed operations, repeatedly applying those operations—no matter how many times—never takes us outside the set. The system remains self-contained and consistent.

Closure of operations appears frequently in software design, even if we don't always recognize it. When used intentionally, it tends to produce simpler and more elegant solutions. For example, strings are closed under concatenation, and the set of all valid HTML documents is closed under DOM to DOM transformations. Closure gives us guarantees that scale from reasoning about tiny functions to designing whole systems by combining closed operations.

This design approach is particularly useful when working with value objects—objects defined by their value rather than by identity. Value objects are typically designed to be closed under their core operations. For example, money can be modeled as a value object: the set of money values is closed under addition and subtraction. The operation never produces an invalid object as long as currencies match, providing equality by value, immutability, encapsulation of invariants, and closure of operations.

Closure of operations is natural for value objects but not typical for entities. Entities are defined by their identity and not by their value, so two entities with identical data are not necessarily the same. Entities have an identity, a lifecycle, change over time, represent a process not a value, and can introduce side effects. The correct design is to compose entities from value objects that are closed under their operations.

An operation can be closed over an abstract type, even if the concrete arguments involved are value objects or entities that are not themselves closed. A good example is the set of extension methods over collections in C# provided by System.Linq. The abstract type IEnumerable<T> is closed under many of its core operations such as Select(), Where(), OrderBy(), Union(), and Distinct().

Whenever possible, define operations whose return type matches the type of their arguments. If a method relies on the state of its object, consider that state as an implicit argument. This creates an opportunity to design the method so that it returns the same type as the object itself. Such operations are closed over the set of instances of that type, which promotes consistency and composability. By identifying closures in code, whether obvious or subtle, and making them explicit, we create designs that are more robust, elegant, and maintainable.

When a set is closed under its allowed operations, repeatedly applying those operations—no matter how many times—never takes us outside the set. The system remains self-contained and consistent.
Closure of operations appears frequently in software design, even if we don't always recognize it. When used intentionally, it tends to produce simpler and more elegant solutions.
This design approach is particularly useful when working with value objects—objects defined by their value rather than by identity.
Entities represent things that happen over time, not things that we compute with.
